data.title: Ex-FIFA president calling for World Cup boycott in US
data.description:
A former president of FIFA is suggesting President Trump’s foreign policy moves are grounds for a boycott of this year’s World Cup, which will hold several matches in major U.S. cities during competition.

Sepp Blatter, in a social media post on Monday, referenced an interview Swiss attorney Mark Pieth gave to a European news outlet in which he urged soccer fans to “stay away from the USA.”

“I think Mark Pieth is right to question this World Cup,” Blatter wrote.

Blatter’s comment was first highlighted by The Associated Press.

President Trump has received praise from current FIFA president Gianni Infantino, who last month presented the president with the first-ever “FIFA Peace Prize,” an award created to celebrate Trump’s diplomatic achievements since taking office.

“And you can always count, Mr. President, on my support, on the support of the entire … soccer community to help you make peace and make the world prosper,” Infantino told the president as he gave him the award during the World Cup’s opening draw in Washington, D.C.

Trump’s moves on the world stage during his second term have roiled some allies, however.

The president threatened sanctions against European allies as he pursues territory in Greenland, fought with Canadian leaders over trade and ordered the capture of Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro.

Some observers have suggested Trump’s foreign policy is hanging over U.S. athletes as they head to Italy for next month’s Winter Olympics, just weeks before major cities like Los Angeles and Atlanta host key World Cup matches.

data.title: Lawler: Collaboration between federal, state, local law enforcement 'should start now'
data.description:
Rep. Mike Lawler (R-N.Y.) in a new opinion piece says Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Customs and Border Protection (CBP) “should reassess their current tactics” after the killing of Alex Pretti Saturday in Minneapolis.

In an opinion piece published by The New York Times on Tuesday, the New York Republican said “collaboration should start now” between federal, state and local officials on investigating Pretti’s dealth.

Administration officials, including Department of Homeland Security Sec. Kristi Noem and Border Patrol leader Greg Bovino, initially described Pretti as a domestic terrorist, though videos of his killing suggest he was not an imminent threat to agents.  

Bovino has been moved out of Minnesota, while Noem had a two-hour meeting at the White House on Monday evening with President Trump, who is reportedly unhappy with how his administration has looked over the last few days.

A number of GOP lawmakers, including Lawler, have offered criticism of ICE’s actions in Minneapolis, even as many have offered support for a policy of deporting migrants illegally in the country.

Lawler urged multi-agency cooperation to “humanely” crack down on illegal immigration in his op-ed.

The Department of Homeland Security (DHS), which oversees both ICE and CBP, said their immigration operation in Minnesota is the largest ever in the agency’s history. 

DHS reportedly deployed over 2,000 federal officers to the state as part of “Operation Metro Surge.” Federal agents’ tactics have been met with extreme resistance from many Minnesota residents, with tensions rising after officers shot and killed Pretti and Renee Good.

State and local law enforcement agencies said they have been shut out of investigations into these two deaths, with DHS most recently denying a signed warrant from a state investigative agency to access the scene where Pretti was fatally shot. 

“The F.B.I. and state and local departments should together investigate the deaths of Renee Good and Alex Pretti,” Lawler said. “A transparent and accountable process that protects the rights of everyone involved, including the deceased and their families, would ensure all relevant evidence is collected and bolster the public’s confidence in immigration enforcement and our justice system.”

Lawler also urged congressional oversight of involved federal agencies over agents’ tactics in Minnesota in order to “promote an honest national conversation about immigration enforcement.”

On Monday, Senate Homeland Security Committee Chair Rand Paul (R-Ky.) sent letters to the heads of ICE, CBP and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services to request that they testify before his committee. 

Lawler said Congress should start working on reforming paths to legal status and citizenship for immigrants. 

“We must be a nation of laws but also one that offers dignity and compassion to those seeking to pursue their American dream,” the congressman wrote. “The events of the past several years show that there will be no Democratic or Republican solution on immigration, only an American one.”

data.title: Business with dictators is a security risk America has fallen for before
data.description:
Recent reporting suggests a push inside the Trump administration for a “peace through business” approach to Russia, premised on the belief that financial ties can provide stability and leverage. Before Washington embraces the notion that commerce can tame hostile autocracies, we should recall the last three times we convinced ourselves of exactly that. 

We tried it with Nazi Germany, China and post-Soviet Russia. Each time, the strategy failed, and the bill came due in the currency nations care about most: national security. 

Consider the most notorious example. By 1938, no one could plausibly ignore the nature of Adolf Hitler’s regime. The Nuremberg Laws had stripped Jews of citizenship. Political opponents filled concentration camps, and Kristallnacht destroyed thousands of Jewish synagogues and businesses. It was covered extensively in U.S. newspapers and State Department cables, just as Russia’s atrocities in Ukraine are today.

Still, several major U.S. corporations — including Standard Oil and the German units of Ford and IBM — maintained commercial relationships that supported Germany’s military and repressive administration. General Motors’ Opel subsidiary produced the “Blitz” truck, which went on to serve as the backbone of the Wehrmacht’s rapid invasion forces.  

After the war, U.S. government investigations concluded that American firms had materially supported the industrial capacity of Hitler’s regime even as its atrocities were well known. The lesson was unmistakable: Economic entanglement strengthened a brutal dictatorship, even as it prepared for a global war in which America would ultimately have to fight.

This history is often framed as a moral indictment, but its core is strategic. Economic integration strengthened a dictatorship’s military power, a mistake the U.S. paid for in blood and treasure — and then repeated, with consequences that define today’s security threats.

Beginning in the 1990s, Washington pursued a bipartisan strategy of deepening economic integration with Beijing. As with Putin’s Russia, policymakers argued that trade, technology transfer and capital flows would liberalize China and moderate its geopolitical ambitions. 

Instead, Beijing used this expansion to build the world’s most comprehensive, repressive surveillance state, militarize the South China Sea and undertake a peacetime military buildup unmatched in scale. U.S. technology and market access accelerated China’s rise to a near-peer competitor intent on displacing America from global leadership. Far from tempering Beijing, economic engagement enabled it.

We made a similar error with post-Soviet Russia. While Washington negotiated the Budapest Memorandum — persuading Ukraine to relinquish the world’s third-largest nuclear arsenal — the U.S. simultaneously funneled billions into Russia through aid packages, privatization programs and International Monetary Fund loans.  

In practice, American-backed privatization helped consolidate strategic industries — energy, minerals, industrial manufacturing — into the hands of oligarchs who became pillars of Vladimir Putin’s regime. The Kremlin repaid America’s investment by launching massive anti-U.S. propaganda, domestic repression and international aggression. In 2019, investigations allege that Russian intelligence even offered bounties to Taliban fighters for killing American soldiers.

Again, engagement did not buy loyalty. It bought the illusion of leverage while Moscow deepened its hostility. These risks are not abstract for Ukrainians. Few nations understand better what happens when powerful dictatorships grow stronger through foreign complacency and investment. Ukraine has lived under Nazi occupation and Stalinist terror — a history embodied in President Volodymyr Zelensky’s own family, many of whom were murdered in the Holocaust. 

Today, some in Washington argue that renewed economic engagement will moderate Moscow — despite everything history teaches. Some contend that reintroducing Western capital could create leverage, encourage cooperation or give Russia a stake in stability.

The opposite is far more likely. Reopening American markets to Russia would weaken sanctions, enrich and modernize Putin’s expansionist war machine, erode deterrence across Europe and Asia, legitimize a hostile dictatorship and teach other autocrats that their brutality can outlast U.S. resolve.

Experience shows that attempts to change the “hearts and minds” of dictatorships through commerce consistently fail. The greatest danger, therefore, comes from supplying such regimes with the resources to act on that hostility — not from constraining them. Integration didn’t moderate Hitler’s Germany, liberalize China or stabilize Russia. It strengthened all three.

Moral arguments also matter. We should reject doing business with a Russian regime orchestrating the largest campaign of state-backed child abductions in Europe since World War II. But even morality is unnecessary to see the national security risk. 

Washington has repeatedly armed future adversaries by treating economic engagement as a form of persuasion rather than a strategic vulnerability. Each time, we eventually faced the consequences when those regimes grew more powerful, more repressive and more capable of projecting hostility abroad. 

Americans do not want their children or grandchildren confronting a stronger authoritarian adversary, especially one empowered by our own capital flows and technology. Yet that is the predictable outcome if we repeat the same mistake with Russia today. When democracies feed autocracies, the strategic costs eventually come home. 

Peace cannot be achieved by enriching regimes that seek to undermine it. And business with dictators is never just business. It is a transfer of power — and we should care very much about whose hands it strengthens. 

Kristina Hook is a nonresident senior fellow at the Atlantic Council and assistant professor of conflict management at Kennesaw State University.

data.title: Florida Republican Vern Buchanan to retire from Congress
data.description:
Rep. Vern Buchanan (R-Fla.) announced on Tuesday that he’s retiring after almost two decades in Congress, making him the third Florida Republican to opt against seeking reelection in the House.

“I came to Congress to solve problems, to fight for working families, and to help ensure this country remains a place where opportunity is available to everyone willing to work for it,” Buchanan, who serves as the No. 2 Republican on the House Ways and Means Committee, said in a statement.

“After 20 years of service, I believe it’s the right time to pass the torch and begin a new chapter in my life,” he added.

Buchanan, who’s served in Congress since 2007, represents Florida’s 16th Congressional District, located in southwest Florida between Tampa and Sarasota. President Trump endorsed the Florida congressman for reelection in November.

Two other Florida Republicans also announced they weren’t seeking reelection: Rep. Neal Dunn (R-Fla.) said he’s retiring after his current term, while Rep. Byron Donalds (R-Fla.) is instead running for Florida governor.

Buchanan’s district went for Trump by over 15 points in 2024, meaning whoever wins the GOP primary is seen as the heavy favorite to win the seat in November.

data.title: What to know about Tom Homan, Trump border czar sent to Minnesota
data.description:
President Trump on Monday announced that White House border czar Tom Homan was headed to Minnesota following another deadly shooting in the state at the hands of federal immigration authorities.

“I am sending Tom Homan to Minnesota tonight. He has not been involved in that area, but knows and likes many of the people there,” Trump said on his Truth Social platform. “Tom is tough but fair and will report to me.”

The Trump administration has been facing heavy backlash in the last few weeks over the fatal shootings of Renee Good by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer and Alex Pretti by a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis. The shootings have also drawn more scrutiny towards the administration’s strict immigration agenda, which has received criticism throughout Trump’s second term.

Here’s what you need to know about Homan as he enters a tense situation in Minnesota:

He has a long history of immigration enforcement

Homan’s career in immigration enforcement stretches back over three decades. Starting law enforcement as a West Carthage, N.Y., police officer, he later joined the Border Patrol, Immigration and Naturalization Service (INS) and ICE.

Beginning in 2013, Homan headed up ICE’s Enforcement and Removal Operations while former President Obama was in office. He had planned to exit public service in 2017 for a consulting role at PriceWaterhouseCoopers.

However, former White House chief of staff John Kelly later offered him ICE’s top position, which Homan held in an acting capacity until 2018.

In 2014, Homan brought up family separations as a way to deter immigration to former Homeland Security Secretary Jeh Johnson under Obama, but Johnson rebuffed the idea, The Atlantic has reported.

But during Trump’s first term, Homan’s family separation idea gained traction, leading to the separation of over 4,000 children from their parents. That tactic was highly controversial and received heavy backlash at the time.

He has helped push Trump 2.0’s strict immigration agenda

Homan rejoined Trump after the then-president-elect in November 2024 announced him as his incoming administration’s “border czar,” tasking the former acting ICE chief with overseeing plans for mass deportations.

Right at the beginning of Trump’s second term, Homan defended immigration raids and vowed to keep working on the enforcement goals of the Trump administration. 

“Look, President Trump won the election on this one issue: securing our border and saving lives. This, what happened on the southern border the last four years, is the biggest national security threat this country’s seen, at least in my lifetime,” Homan said in January 2025.

Homan has also helped promote the administration’s threats of strict immigration enforcement in Democratic-led cities and targeted so-called “sanctuary cities,” or cities with laws on the books restricting cooperation with federal authorities on immigration enforcement.

“President Trump’s prioritized sanctuary cities because sanctuary cities knowingly release illegal alien public safety threats to the streets every day. That’s where the problem is.” Homan said in September.

Earlier this month, Homan defended the ICE officer who fatally shot Renee Good, saying the officer thought he “did the right thing.”

“I truly believe, and again, this will come out in the investigation, that in his mind, he feared for his life, and he took appropriate action,” Homan told NBC News’s Kristen Welker on “Meet the Press.”

More recently — prior to the killing of Pretti — he said said the Trump administration needs to be better about its “messaging” over the ICE operations across the country.

“Look, bottom line is: 70 percent of everybody arrested is a criminal,” Homan said on “The Ingraham Angle” on Fox News. “We need to start advertising that every single day and putting pictures all over social media.”

He has recently faced controversy over a bribery scandal

MS NOW reported last year that, according to internal documents and multiple people involved in an investigation, Homan had been recorded taking $50,000 in 2024 from FBI agents pretending to be businesspeople. The outlet also reported that the Homan took the cash in exchange for indicating he could assist the agents in winning government contracts.

Homan has shot down allegations that he took the bribe. 

“I didn’t take $50,000 from anybody,” Homan said in October.

data.title: Live updates: Senate returns amid DHS funding uproar as shutdown deadline looms
data.description:
The Senate heads back to Washington on Tuesday as a partial government shutdown looms. But Democrats are eyeing funding for Department of Homeland Security (DHS), after the weekend killing of a Minneapolis man by a Border Patrol (CBP) agent.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) has indicated he intends to move forward with a six-bill funding package, which includes DHS, despite Democrats’ threats to block the measure. Thune must confront whether he can rally his conference and the White House around a modified bill that separates DHS funding from the package to avoid another shutdown.

It also comes as President Trump is making adjustments to his signature initiatives in the wake of Alex Pretti’s death on Saturday. Border czar Tom Homan will arrive Tuesday in Minneapolis, replacing Border Patrol commander Greg Bovino.

The shooting ignited a firestorm on both sides of the aisle, with Democrats calling for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to be fired or impeached, and Republican lawmakers expressing grave concern and calling for hearings.

Trump on Tuesday will head to Des Moines, Iowa, to deliver remarks.

Follow live below.

data.title: ICE officers to have Olympics security role: Report
data.description:
Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers are expected to help with security at the Milano-Cortina Winter Games in Italy.

Agents will join the international band of law enforcement securing the Olympic event, according to two officials at the U.S. Embassy in Rome, The Associated Press reported.

However, Italian officials have decried ICE’s presence in the midst of national outrage after the fatal shootings of Alex Pretti and Renee Good at the hands of federal agents in Minnesota this month amid President Trump’s crackdown on illegal immigration.

“This is a militia that kills, a militia that enters into the homes of people, signing their own permission slips,” Milan Mayor Giuseppe Sala said during an interview with RTL Radio 102, according to the AP. “It is clear they are not welcome in Milan, without a doubt.”

His comments were made prior to the confirmation of ICE’s attendance at the games, first reported by the Italian daily il Fatto Quotidiano.

Italian Interior Minister Matteo Piantedosi on Saturday said he doesn’t “see what the problem would be” if ICE officers helped to secure the games, AP reported.

In previous years, the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) sent its investigative unit, the Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), to provide security support during the Olympics, according to the news wire.

data.title: Trump approval on immigration at record low: Survey
data.description:
President Trump’s approval ratings on immigration have dropped to a record low, according to a new poll.

The Reuters/Ipsos survey results come during the Trump administration’s massive immigration crackdown operations in Minnesota, where tensions have risen to a boiling point after federal agents shot and killed two people and wounded another man. 

Only 39 percent of respondents in the Monday survey said they approved of Trump’s immigration policies, and 53 percent said they disapproved. 

While this is the lowest approval rating yet for the president on this issue, it follows a recent downward trend in polling on the topic. 

In a Reuters/Ipsos survey conducted earlier this month, 40 percent of respondents said they approved and 52 percent disapproved of his administration’s immigration policies. In the president’s first administration, his lowest approval rating on immigration was also 40 percent. 

The Biden administration’s worst approval rating on this issue was 26 percent. The former president hit this record twice, in May 2023 and December 2024. 

Trump made immigration a central component of his presidential campaigns, promising to crack down on illegal immigration into the U.S. 

However, immigration officers’ tactics in Minnesota — and the latest crackdown in Maine — have raised eyebrows from some Republicans lawmakers, not to mention drawn intense backlash from congressional Democrats. 

Following the second fatal shooting of a Minnesota resident by federal officials, Senate Homeland Security Committee Chair Rand Paul (R-Ky.) on Monday requested testimony from the leaders of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), Customs and Border Patrol and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services in letters to each agency.

Additionally, Democratic senators said they will not support a crucial funding package that allocates money to the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), including $10 billion more to ICE. 

On Monday, Republicans shot down a request from Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer to remove the provisions related to DHS. This process would have involved calling back the House into session to vote on the amended package. 

If the Senate does not pass the funding package by the deadline this Friday, the government will partially shut down. 

Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) said in posts on the social platform X on Monday that he spoke with the president who “agreed the present situation can’t continue.” Minneapolis has been the site of both fatal shootings and mass protests against federal law enforcement’s operations in the state. 

Frey said federal agents will begin “leaving the area” on Tuesday and that he will meet with Trump’s border czar, Tom Homan, on the same day. 

The Reuters/Ipsos poll gathered responses from 1,139 Americans from Jan. 23-25 and had a margin of error of plus or minus 3 percentage points.

data.title: If the Trump administration can't trust Tulsi Gabbard, who can?
data.description:
In the final hours before the U.S. operation to capture Venezuelan dictator Nicolas Maduro, President Trump was surrounded by a cadre of trusted advisers — with one noteworthy absence.

Tulsi Gabbard, the Director of National Intelligence, was doing sun salutations on a beach in Hawaii. She had been routinely excluded from Venezuela operational planning for so long, in fact, that White House aides reportedly joked that “DNI” really stands for “Do Not Invite.” 

Gabbard’s exclusion may stem from her extensive history of opposing a hardline policy against the Maduro dictatorship, once writing that intervention would “wreak death and destruction on the Venezuelan people.” While the final death toll of the operation is still uncertain, assessments have found that fewer than 100 people died, of which a plurality were Cuban security personnel.

This raises a fundamental question: If the Trump administration does not trust its own Director of National Intelligence with a highly sensitive and crucial operation, how can the American people — or, for that matter, America’s intelligence-sharing partners? 

Although administration officials, including Vice President JD Vance, have formally denied Gabbard’s absence, it was far from inconspicuous. Dramatic photos from the Situation Room published by the White House prominently feature Secretary of State Marco Rubio, Homeland Security Adviser Stephen Miller and CIA Director John Ratcliffe flanking the president. Gabbard broke her silence on the operation over 48 hours later, praising the operation while noticeably omitting the words “Maduro” or “Venezuela.”

Although a State Department spokesman has denied that it was Rubio who was responsible for sidelining Gabbard, the two have a history of sparring on foreign policy during their time in Congress. In a pointed X post from 2015, for example, Gabbard criticized Rubio and the late Sen. John McCain (R-Ariz.) for opposing Russian strikes in Syria, an operation that quickly devolved into a years-long campaign of Russian planes bombing Syrian hospitals in partnership with the now-deposed Assad regime.  

Gabbard’s isolation came to a head after a messy confirmation process, which included a statement where Gabbard felt it necessary to refute that she was a “puppet” of America’s adversaries, like Assad or Vladimir Putin. The fact that she failed to earn the faith on that account of the very administration that nominated her is a stunning indictment of her capability as Director of National Intelligence. 

Instead of transparent denials, the American people deserve to know exactly why the person charged with overseeing 18 intelligence agencies was not included in one of the most consequential operations conducted by the Trump administration so far. At a time when America’s relationships with its allies are already tenuous, most recently due to abrasive comments on NATO and Greenland, Gabbard’s questionable status will certainly compound already-existing concerns from intelligence-sharing partners, including the crucial Five Eyes alliance. 

During Gabbard’s confirmation process, British security officials voiced concerns about further intelligence-sharing with the U.S. due to Gabbard’s years of apologetics for Putin. Mere months later, Gabbard issued a directive to U.S. intelligence agencies to bar Five Eyes Partners — including the United Kingdom — from accessing information on Russia-Ukraine negotiations. 

The U.S. faces a litany of short-and long-term challenges that require a competent leader guiding the intelligence community.

On Iran, Gabbard was similarly sidelined during discussions about potential military action in June, due to a bizarre video she unilaterally released on social media warning of impending nuclear war, as well as dovish comments made while Gabbard was a Democratic congresswoman.

On Russia, Gabbard has a sordid history of apologetics, including once authoring a memo to her congressional staffers that decried America’s “hostility toward Putin.” 

On China, Gabbard has repeatedly boosted talking points originating from Beijing to attack Japan, a key American ally. This has included bemoaning efforts in Tokyo to boost Japan’s defense capabilities — efforts the Trump administration itself has repeatedly encouraged.

One year into the job, Gabbard has proven time and time again that she is out of lockstep with the Trump administration, America’s partners and the American people. Her exclusion from the Venezuela operation was not the first time she has been sidelined — and it certainly will not be the last.

Kareem Rifai is a graduate student at the Georgetown Security Studies program.

data.title: Former ICE director: Trump will take 'next step' if Minnesota leaders don't show commitment to law and order
data.description:
Former Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) Acting Director Jonathan Fahey said Monday that President Trump may have to “take the next step” if local and state leaders in Minnesota don’t “show that they’re committed to law and order” amid protests over several recent shootings involving federal immigration officers.

“A lot of this action by these so-called protestors, they really are just acting in subversive ways, and they’re clearly coordinated, and they’re not to speak out against a policy,” Fahey told NewsNation’s Katie Pavlich. “They’re there to disrupt the enforcement of law, which is the very essence of our democracy.”

“I think today is a great opportunity to leaders in Minnesota to either show that they’re committed to law and order, and if they’re not, I think President Trump will take the next step, which is not going to be to leave Minnesota, but it may be the Insurrection Act or something else,” he added.

Protests have intensified across Minneapolis following the fatal shooting of 37-year-old Alex Pretti, a U.S. citizen and ICU nurse, by a U.S. Border Patrol agent over the weekend. It was the third shooting involving federal officers since Operation Metro Surge began late last month.

Trump initially threatened to invoke the Insurrection Act in response to growing unrest over the presence of federal law enforcement officers in the state and criticism over the administration’s tactics, but later said there was no reason to use it “right now.”

The White House has shifted its tone in the wake of Pretti’s death, which sparked bipartisan outrage, with Trump pledging to pull some Border Patrol agents out of the state following a phone call with Gov. Tim Walz (D).

“Governor Tim Walz called me with the request to work together with respect to Minnesota. It was a very good call, and we, actually, seemed to be on a similar wavelength,” Trump wrote Monday on Truth Social.

Border Patrol commander-at-large Greg Bovino, who claimed Pretti had approached officers with a gun and characterized him as an “assassin”, is also expected to leave Minnesota on Tuesday. Border czar Tom Homan is set to take his place overseeing the mission and report directly to the president.

Fahey said Monday that Homan “brings a lot of credibility to the issue.”

“If these leaders continue to defy, continue to obstruct after he gets in there, it’s very telling where they’re coming from,” he added.

Video of the incident appeared to show an agent confiscating Pretti’s gun, which he had a license to carry, before another agent shot multiple rounds at him. Republicans and Democrats have called for a thorough investigation into the incident.  

News of Bovino’s exit did not stop protestors from demonstrating outside the hotel where he was believed to be staying on Monday night. Local police said the “situation escalated,” with some throwing objects at officers and engaging in other unlawful behavior and dispersed the crowd.

Several people were taken into custody, according to the Minnesota Star Tribune.
